title: generate systemd pod
body:
generate systemd: support pods

Support generating systemd unit files for a pod.  Podman generates one
unit file for the pod including the PID file for the infra container's
conmon process and one unit file for each container (excluding the infra
container).

Note that this change implies refactorings in the `pkg/systemdgen` API.

The output of a simple pod with an alpine container looks as follows:
```
[Unit]
Description=5d85e91228e02b0fca3982e6db9a0458a381eeffca7951d06f415d6a6495fa29 Podman Pod
Documentation=man:podman-generate-systemd(1)
Requires=container-63202b5664827c0922807cb62ea7ec5c931faabca2c730e5baa29f201bab3596.service
Before=container-63202b5664827c0922807cb62ea7ec5c931faabca2c730e5baa29f201bab3596.service

[Service]
Restart=on-failure
ExecStart=/home/valentin/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman pod start 5d85e91228e02b0fca3982e6db9a0458a381eeffca7951d06f415d6a6495fa29
ExecStop=/home/valentin/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman pod stop  5d85e91228e02b0fca3982e6db9a0458a381eeffca7951d06f415d6a6495fa29
KillMode=none
Type=forking
PIDFile=/tmp/1000/overlay-containers/ae2965240e1ed93064d7145373346d0461c9474215837d364b830bafc7106006/userdata/conmon.pid

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

[Unit]
Description=63202b5664827c0922807cb62ea7ec5c931faabca2c730e5baa29f201bab3596 Podman Container
Documentation=man:podman-generate-systemd(1)
PartOf=pod-5d85e91228e02b0fca3982e6db9a0458a381eeffca7951d06f415d6a6495fa29.service.service

[Service]
Restart=on-failure
ExecStart=/home/valentin/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman start 63202b5664827c0922807cb62ea7ec5c931faabca2c730e5baa29f201bab3596
ExecStop=/home/valentin/go/src/github.com/containers/libpod/bin/podman -t 10 63202b5664827c0922807cb62ea7ec5c931faabca2c730e5baa29f201bab3596
KillMode=none
Type=forking
PIDFile=/tmp/1000/overlay-containers/63202b5664827c0922807cb62ea7ec5c931faabca2c730e5baa29f201bab3596/userdata/conmon.pid

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <rothberg@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *vrothberg* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [vrothberg] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

@mheon @baude @rhatdan @edsantiago @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL

Note, that the native client can now also generate files.

Minor nits:
* The '`Requires=` and `Before=` lines contain trailing whitespace. (I realize this is a preexisting condition; I'm just mentioning it in case it's easy to fix)
* The generated files do not include a trailing newline. This should be easy to fix.

> Minor nits:
> 
>     * The '`Requires=` and `Before=` lines contain trailing whitespace. (I realize this is a preexisting condition; I'm just mentioning it in case it's easy to fix)

That's actually introduced with this PR. I try to fix that ... go templates can be tricky. Thanks a lot for checking!

I will work on a few more unit tests and do some templating clean ups. In the meantime, I want to make sure that pod/container unit files make sense and that I didn't miss something ... @edsantiago @baude @mheon do they make sense to you? Ideally, could you play around and test the generated files?
We do have a systemd daemon available in "most" of the test VMs (exception is Podman-in-Podman). However, it's global state - interacting with it from threaded tests is inherently risky.

> We do have a systemd daemon available in "most" of the test VMs (exception is Podman-in-Podman). However, it's global state - interacting with it from threaded tests is inherently risky.

I share the thought but I also feel that actually testing the files exceeds the PR.
Agree wholeheartedly. If we want to add testing, that sounds like a separate card and PR.

A few more thoughts:
* If the argument to `systemd generate` is a pod _name_, why not use that as the filename (and Description, ExecStart, etc) instead of the sha? Similarly if the container(s) have user-specified names.
* Did you consider writing the systemd files into their respective places (`/etc/systemd/system` or `$HOME/.config/systemd`)? I wouldn't necessarily recommend this, it makes me uncomfortable, I'm just wondering if you weighed the pros and cons.
* On autogenerated files, I like adding a comment at top showing a timestamp and 'autogenerated by COMMAND'.
* I can't actually get it to work as rootless: 
```
$ systemctl --user status container-59767e886456494857f68c14b4aa71a5b32917ebb42ebcf4ecd4a4c278c3b5a2.service | cat
Failed to start 59767e886456494857f68c14b4aa71a5b32917ebb42ebcf4ecd4a4c278c3b5a2 Podman Container
$ [ pod ]
pod-b7f9874412af55c8425187fc334aea8b565d49cb910c872f13a2fe0fef455c05.service: Start request repeated too quickly.
```
```

> A few more thoughts:
> 
>     * If the argument to `systemd generate` is a pod _name_, why not use that as the filename (and Description, ExecStart, etc) instead of the sha? Similarly if the container(s) have user-specified names.

That's possible when using the `--name` flag. Then we use the format `{container,pod}-name.service`.

>     * Did you consider writing the systemd files into their respective places (`/etc/systemd/system` or `$HOME/.config/systemd`)? I wouldn't necessarily recommend this, it makes me uncomfortable, I'm just wondering if you weighed the pros and cons.

Yes, I was thinking about writing the files to some configurable default but I prefer users to take care of that.

>     * On autogenerated files, I like adding a comment at top showing a timestamp and 'autogenerated by COMMAND'.

That's a great suggestion! Will do that.

>     * I can't actually get it to work as rootless:
> 
> 
> ```
> $ systemctl --user status container-59767e886456494857f68c14b4aa71a5b32917ebb42ebcf4ecd4a4c278c3b5a2.service | cat
> Failed to start 59767e886456494857f68c14b4aa71a5b32917ebb42ebcf4ecd4a4c278c3b5a2 Podman Container
> $ [ pod ]
> pod-b7f9874412af55c8425187fc334aea8b565d49cb910c872f13a2fe0fef455c05.service: Start request repeated too quickly.
> ```

Thanks a ton for checking. I've seen this error during development but didn't hit it after. Can you share the pod/containers you used?

Can you clarify "pod/containers you used"? I followed your trivial example 'foo' and 'alpine top'.

One thing I'm seeing is that the `conmon.pid` file is missing even though conmon is running and 'podman ps' lists the container running; I wonder if systemd is deleting it in certain circumstances? I'm continuing to investigate but wanted to share this early.

> Can you clarify "pod/containers you used"? I followed your trivial example 'foo' and 'alpine top'.

I tested the `foo` pod with a varying number of `alpine top` containers.

> One thing I'm seeing is that the `conmon.pid` file is missing even though conmon is running and 'podman ps' lists the container running; I wonder if systemd is deleting it in certain circumstances? I'm continuing to investigate but wanted to share this early.

That's very odd and screams for adding unit tests for the pod case.

@vrothberg try this for a container:
```
$ podman create --pod foo alpine sh -c 'echo Starting;while :;do date;sleep 1;done'
```
I'm seeing it restart every ten seconds (via `podman logs -f`); which looks suspiciously like the `-t 10` in the `ExecStop` but I'm not having luck figuring out why `stop` would be getting called.

I think this might be it. From `journalctl --user -xe`:
```
container-vibrant_cartwright.service: New main PID 24754 does not belong to service, and PID file is not owned by root. Refusing.
```
and, from `systemd.service(5)` under `PIDFile=`:
```
    The PID file does not need to be owned by a
    privileged user, but if it is owned by an unprivileged user additional safety
    restrictions are enforced: the file may not be a symlink to a file owned by a
    different user (neither directly nor indirectly), and the PID file must refer to
    a process already belonging to the service.
```
Could this be resolved by the requested `no-cgroup` option? And if so should I stop trying to test nonroot systemd, and in fact should you even restrict/disable the possibility of using `generate systemd` as nonroot?
`no-cgroup` would probably resolve this.

Looking into it now, thanks @edsantiago!

The headers now look like that:
```
# container-ced2fb140f0cfeb1c8fa21c2397b4d1ac1f48622265fdc7b4a759e5ff9b285fa.service
# autogenerated by Podman 1.5.1-dev
# Wed Aug 14 19:42:20 CEST 2019
```

vrothberg@irc> now I am hitting cases where the conmon.pid is missing for real.

I think that one of the cases I saw of missing-pidfile was because I was also manually playing with podman commands. systemd does not like this.

Thinking aloud: what if the generated unit files all used `podman --runroot /somewhere/else` (and possibly `--root` also)? This could isolate systemd-managed pods & containers.

(For the record I think the logistical hassles and complexity make this an unmanageable option; I'm throwing it out simply for the sake of possibility)
@edsantiago, if you have some cycles, I'd appreciate your thoughts and feedback on the latest state.

Generated files are still missing the final newline, could I insist on getting that fixed? 

Apart from that, it looks really good: it works almost exactly as expected. The only unexpected behavior is:
```
$ systemctl --user start pod-foo.service
$ podman ps
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                            COMMAND               CREATED        STATUS           PORTS  NAMES
299f30b7b792  docker.io/library/alpine:latest  sh -c echo Start2...  2 minutes ago  Up 1 second ago         angry_shannon
67e686bacd06  docker.io/library/alpine:latest  sh -c echo Start1...  2 minutes ago  Up 1 second ago         quizzical_dewdney

$ podman stop  67e
67e686bacd0660d0f152737694bef4abf4dcf3cfa357988eda3b6cd167a52fe1

$ systemctl --user status container-quizzical_dewdney.service
● container-quizzical_dewdney.service - Podman container-quizzical_dewdney.service
   Loaded: loaded (/home/esm/.config/systemd/user/container-quizzical_dewdney.service; disabled; vendor preset: enabled)
   Active: inactive (dead) since Tue 2019-08-20 11:32:44 MDT; 37s ago
     Docs: man:podman-generate-systemd(1)
  Process: 2136 ExecStart=/home/esm/src/atomic/2018-02.podman/libpod/bin/podman start quizzical_dewdney (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
  Process: 2402 ExecStop=/home/esm/src/atomic/2018-02.podman/libpod/bin/podman stop -t 10 quizzical_dewdney (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
 Main PID: 2194 (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)

Aug 20 11:32:24 f.edsantiago.com systemd[1319]: Starting Podman container-quizzical_dewdney.service...
Aug 20 11:32:24 f.edsantiago.com podman[2136]: 2019-08-20 11:32:24.92576536 -0600 MDT m=+0.260550371 container init 67e686bacd0660d0f152737694bef4abf4dcf3cfa35>
Aug 20 11:32:24 f.edsantiago.com podman[2136]: 2019-08-20 11:32:24.934971566 -0600 MDT m=+0.269756578 container start 67e686bacd0660d0f152737694bef4abf4dcf3cfa>
Aug 20 11:32:24 f.edsantiago.com podman[2136]: quizzical_dewdney
Aug 20 11:32:24 f.edsantiago.com systemd[1319]: Started Podman container-quizzical_dewdney.service.
Aug 20 11:32:44 f.edsantiago.com podman[2194]: 2019-08-20 11:32:44.125152856 -0600 MDT m=+0.158152884 container cleanup 67e686bacd0660d0f152737694bef4abf4dcf3c>
Aug 20 11:32:44 f.edsantiago.com podman[2402]: 67e686bacd0660d0f152737694bef4abf4dcf3cfa357988eda3b6cd167a52fe1
Aug 20 11:32:44 f.edsantiago.com systemd[1319]: container-quizzical_dewdney.service: Succeeded.
```
That is: I started the service, stopped one container (using podman, behind systemd's back). I was expecting that container to restart. Is my expectation wrong?

Really nice work. That was a good idea with the infra container.
@rhatdan you happy with this one?

> Generated files are still missing the final newline, could I insist on getting that fixed?

Yes, please insist :) I missed that one.

> [...]
> That is: I started the service, stopped one container (using podman, behind systemd's back). I was expecting that container to restart. Is my expectation wrong?

Your expectation is correct. In order for this to work, we need an updated conmon with https://github.com/containers/conmon/commit/74da3fb06d4444aca4724bddc8e61729600a4d81. I noticed the same issue and we tracked it down to old conmons not returning the exit code of the container which ultimately lets systemd believe that everything's in order.

> Really nice work. That was a good idea with the infra container.

Thanks! I am very happy that `podman pod ps` etc. continues working although not being started via `podman pod start`.

I am happy with the current state. As discussed, the support for the remote client has been dropped entirely.

@edsantiago @baude @mheon @rhatdan ready for final review.

@edsantiago You expectations are incorrect for now.  We had talked a while back about adding the feature to a pod to specify what happens when a container exits.  There should be three possible configurations.
1.  Nothing.   The pod and all other containers continue running
2.  Restart.  The container is automatically restarted
3.  Shutdown.  The pod and all other containers are stopped.

I would like to see this implemented.   Not sure if this needs to be tied to systemd.  I think making this a basic feature of pods would be nice.

@rhatdan, I think that's an orthogonal topic. In systemd-service terms, the service should be restarted when we do a `podman stop $ctr` since it should have a non-zero exit code, was stopped via a signal and the service is configured to restart on-failure.

1) and 2) are working already with the patched version of conmon.

> 3) Shutdown. The pod and all other containers are stopped.

This will be trickier to map into systemd terms but should be possible when we alter the dependency graph.
Corrected the typos, thanks for checking @rhatdan!
Thanks for checking, @TomSweeneyRedHat! I will fix with the next push but am waiting for more feedback to avoid wasting energy with redundant CI runs.
Documentation nits only; all else LGTM. Thanks for your perseverance.
Thanks for the review, @edsantiago and @TomSweeneyRedHat! I just updated the PR.
LGTM, nice work @vrothberg
/hold
/lgtm
@rhatdan drop the hold if you happy with the pr now
/hold cancel
